scenario: Vertex Bank runs a loan-review workflow in Camunda 8.8 with 2500 user tasks per day in Tasklist. A reviewer self-assigns an urgent task from the shared queue, then realises a colleague with regional expertise should handle it instead, and wants to release the task without forcing completion.
question: Which Tasklist action lets the reviewer release the self-assigned task back to the queue?

options.text: The reviewer can unassign the task at any time before completion, which returns it to the queue so another Tasklist user can pick it up and complete it later
The reviewer must complete the task once she has self-assigned it; Tasklist does not expose an unassign action after a user task has been claimed from the queue
The reviewer must ask a Tasklist administrator to remove her assignment; end users themselves cannot release a user task that has already been claimed from the queue
The reviewer can transfer the task to a named colleague via a direct reassignment action, which overwrites the original BPMN assignment with the new Tasklist user reference

a.text: Correct. The Tasklist docs state that once a task is assigned to a user, the user can unassign it if they do not intend to work on it, returning the task to the queue.

b.text: Incorrect. Tasklist explicitly allows the assignee to unassign a claimed task; completion is not the only exit path for an assigned user task in the Camunda 8 docs.

c.text: Incorrect. End users can release their own assigned tasks directly in Tasklist; no administrator intervention is documented for the standard unassign action on a user task.

d.text: Incorrect. Tasklist does not document a direct user-to-user transfer action; the assignee unassigns the task and another user then claims it from the shared queue instead.

explanation: When a user task in Tasklist is assigned, the assignee can either complete it or unassign it. Unassigning returns the task to the queue so another user can pick it up and finish the work.
